quality_gate_exclusions: GPMelt (Gaussian process framework for dark meltome)
GSimp and QRILC proteomics imputation methods

C1-H1.title: GEV Tail Index (xi) as Phylogenomic Signature of Thermal Adaptation Strategy

partial_confirmations.claim: Thermophile proteome Tm distributions are narrower (smaller spread/SD) than mesophile distributions, consistent with tail truncation (Weibull domain, xi < 0)
partial_confirmations.paper: Oztug et al. 2020 — Thermal Proteome Profiling and Meltome Analysis of a Thermophilic Bacterial Strain, Geobacillus thermoleovorans ARTRW1
partial_confirmations.specific_support: Reports G. thermoleovorans Tm mean 68.1 +/- 6.6 degrees C (SD = 6.6) vs E. coli 90% range 20 degrees C in Jarzab 2020. Narrower thermophile distribution is consistent with Weibull-domain tail truncation predicted by C1-H1.

partial_confirmations.claim: GEV/GPD shape parameter (xi or kappa) can classify biological distributions into Weibull, Gumbel, or Frechet domain — the FTG-based classification framework applies to biological data
partial_confirmations.paper: Joyce et al. 2008 — A General Extreme Value Theory Model for the Adaptation of DNA Sequences Under Strong Selection and Weak Mutation
partial_confirmations.specific_support: Uses GPD peaks-over-threshold to classify distributions of beneficial fitness effects. Demonstrates that biological data (viral fitness) falls in Weibull domain (kappa approx -1), and derives predictions robust across all three FTG domains. First demonstration that EVT domain classification (Weibull/Gumbel/Frechet) applies to a biological distributional problem — directly validates the mathematical sub-mechanism underlying C1-H1.

partial_confirmations.claim: EVT shape parameter differentiates biological populations across different selective regimes — GEV xi varies systematically with biological environment
partial_confirmations.paper: Beisel et al. 2007 — Testing the Extreme Value Domain of Attraction for Distributions of Beneficial Fitness Effects
partial_confirmations.specific_support: Develops likelihood framework specifically for testing which EVT domain (Weibull/Gumbel/Frechet) a biological distribution falls in. Shows populations with different fitness landscapes have distinguishable EVT domain classifications. This is the key methodological sub-mechanism of C1-H1 — applying EVT domain testing to biological distributions — confirmed in a different (evolutionary genetics) domain.

partial_confirmations.claim: Meltome-type proteome Tm distributions from additional species show distributions with distinct shape characteristics — raw material for cross-species GEV fitting exists beyond original 13-species atlas
partial_confirmations.paper: Meltome Atlas of Arabidopsis thaliana proteome (2025)
partial_confirmations.specific_support: Reports Arabidopsis proteome Tm index ranging from -15.6 to 9.6 with three distinct groups. Provides an additional species dataset for cross-species GEV fitting. Does not use EVT analysis, but extends the empirical base for testing C1-H1 beyond the 13-species Meltome Atlas.

C1-H1.summary: No active clinical trials, funded grants, or patents directly address the GEV tail index classification of thermal adaptation strategies. However, four partial mechanism confirmations are found: (1) thermophile meltome data (Oztug 2020) shows narrower Tm distributions consistent with Weibull-domain tail truncation; (2-3) evolutionary genetics literature (Joyce 2008; Beisel 2007) demonstrates EVT domain classification working in biological systems using the same GPD peaks-over-threshold framework, confirming the key mathematical sub-mechanism; (4) a 2025 Arabidopsis meltome atlas extends the empirical base for cross-species GEV fitting. The hypothesis remains disjoint — no prior work applies this to proteome Tm distributions — but the sub-mechanisms have independent biological precedents.

C1-H2.title: Complex-Minimum Tm Return Levels Predict Process-Specific Thermal Failure Temperatures

clinical_trials.title: Hyperthermia Combined With Immunotherapy in the Treatment of Cancer
clinical_trials.note: Tests hyperthermia as cancer treatment but does not use EVT return levels or protein complex failure temperature prediction as endpoint.

patents.title: Methods to identify protein interaction (Proximity Co-Aggregation / MS-CETSA)
patents.assignee: Agency for Science Technology and Research Singapore
patents.note: Patents the TPCA methodology that underlies the complex-minimum Tm approach in C1-H2. Claims proteome-wide assessment of co-aggregation using Euclidean distance metrics. Does not claim EVT return-level analysis of complex-minimum Tm distributions, so the specific C1-H2 innovation (EVT return levels on Tm_min) is not covered.

partial_confirmations.claim: Protein complexes in specific cellular processes (ribosome, proteasome) show systematic co-melting behavior allowing characterization of complex-level thermal signatures
partial_confirmations.paper: Sun et al. 2023 — Improved in situ characterization of protein complex dynamics at scale with thermal proximity co-aggregation (Slim-TPCA)
partial_confirmations.specific_support: Shows that ribosomal complexes (40S/60S) and the 20S proteasome have distinctive co-melting signatures under glucose deprivation stress. Confirms that specific pathway-level complexes have characteristic thermal profiles distinguishable from proteome background. This is the key empirical sub-mechanism underlying C1-H2: complex-level thermal coherence enabling process-specific thermal failure prediction.

partial_confirmations.claim: Thermally vulnerable proteins cluster within macromolecular complexes rather than being randomly distributed across the proteome
partial_confirmations.paper: Thermal Proteome Profiling Reveals Meltome Upon NLRP3 Inflammasome Activation (2025)
partial_confirmations.specific_support: 2025 study reports that the majority of thermally vulnerable proteins tend to cluster into distinct macromolecular complexes upon NLRP3 activation. This independently confirms that complex membership predicts thermal vulnerability — the foundational sub-mechanism of C1-H2.

partial_confirmations.claim: Large-scale proteome-wide thermal shift assays can systematically characterize thermal behavior of hundreds of protein complexes simultaneously
partial_confirmations.paper: Large-scale characterization of drug mechanism of action using proteome-wide thermal shift assays (eLife 2024)
partial_confirmations.specific_support: Reports 1.4 million thermal stability measurements across 96 compounds, showing that protein complexes show coordinated thermal stability responses. Demonstrates scalability of the measurement approach. Does not use EVT return levels, but confirms the empirical infrastructure for C1-H2's complex-level analysis.

C1-H2.summary: One adjacent clinical trial (hyperthermia + immunotherapy, not mechanism-specific) and one adjacent patent (TPCA methodology, WO2019035773A1 — does not claim EVT return levels). Three partial mechanism confirmations: Slim-TPCA (2023) confirms complex-level co-melting for ribosomes and proteasomes; NLRP3 meltome 2025 confirms thermally vulnerable proteins cluster in complexes; eLife 2024 large-scale PISA confirms measurement scalability. The patent confirms the TPCA methodology is considered commercially valuable, suggesting the infrastructure underlying C1-H2 is already protected — but the EVT return-level innovation itself (the MAGELLAN contribution) is not patented.

C1-H7.title: GPD Scale Parameter Predicts Evolutionary Rate in the Thermally Vulnerable Subproteome

partial_confirmations.claim: Protein thermal stability directly affects dN/dS evolutionary rate — lower stability correlates with faster or altered evolutionary rates
partial_confirmations.paper: Dasmeh et al. 2014 — The Influence of Selection for Protein Stability on dN/dS Estimations
partial_confirmations.specific_support: Demonstrates through simulation and analysis that protein folding stability directly influences dN/dS estimates. At low folding stability, deviations from neutrality appear including per-site dN/dS > 1 (compensatory mutations) and dN/dS < 1 (purifying selection). Directly validates the sub-mechanism linking thermal stability to evolutionary rate that C1-H7 generalizes using GPD.

partial_confirmations.claim: There is a theoretical and empirical framework linking protein stability constraints to evolutionary rates across the proteome
partial_confirmations.paper: Joyce et al. 2008 — A General Extreme Value Theory Model for the Adaptation of DNA Sequences Under Strong Selection and Weak Mutation
partial_confirmations.specific_support: Shows that EVT (specifically GPD peaks-over-threshold) applies to evolutionary genetics problems. The Weibull domain dominance in fitness effects (kappa < 0) means that extreme phenotypes (very low or very high fitness) are bounded — directly analogous to the bounded lower tail of Tm distributions hypothesized in C1-H7.

partial_confirmations.claim: Highly expressed proteins are more thermally stable and evolve more slowly — expression mediates stability-evolution relationship
partial_confirmations.paper: Drummond et al. 2005 — Why Highly Expressed Proteins Evolve Slowly
partial_confirmations.specific_support: Confirms that expression level explains roughly half the variation in S. cerevisiae protein evolutionary rates, and highly expressed proteins (which Leuenberger 2017 shows are more thermally stable) evolve slower. This validates the sub-mechanism: thermally vulnerable (low-stability, low-expression) proteins face different selective pressures. Note: This is the correct citation for the PNAS paper that was mislabeled in the pipeline.

partial_confirmations.claim: Temperature itself affects evolutionary rate — thermal environment shapes selection on protein sequences
partial_confirmations.paper: Knopp et al. 2024 — High temperature delays and low temperature accelerates evolution of a new protein phenotype
partial_confirmations.specific_support: Experimental evolution shows that cooling accelerates, and warming decelerates, the evolution of a new protein phenotype (yellow fluorescence). Mechanism: protein-destabilizing mutations required for neofunctionalization are less tolerated at high temperature. This directly supports the C1-H7 sub-mechanism: thermally vulnerable proteins (low Tm) face stronger constraints because destabilizing mutations are more lethal in high-temperature environments.

C1-H7.summary: No active clinical trials, funded grants, or patents for GPD-scale prediction of evolutionary rate in thermally vulnerable proteins. Four partial mechanism confirmations: Dasmeh 2014 validates the stability-dN/dS sub-mechanism; Joyce 2008 validates GPD peaks-over-threshold in biological evolutionary context; Drummond 2005 PNAS validates the expression-stability-evolution triangle; Knopp 2024 Nature Commun provides experimental evidence that temperature shapes evolutionary rate through protein stability. Together these confirm the component sub-mechanisms of C1-H7 (thermal stability to evolutionary rate) have strong independent support, even though the specific GPD-based quantification of the tail vulnerability zone has not been attempted.

aggregate.notes: All three hypotheses show CONVERGENT_MODERATE. No hypothesis reaches CONVERGENT_STRONG (which requires an active clinical trial or funded grant directly testing the mechanism). The adjacent patent WO2019035773A1 covers TPCA methodology but not EVT return-level analysis, meaning the core MAGELLAN innovation for C1-H2 is not yet patented.
